document: Anchovies of the species Thryssa cuvierii and Thryssa mystax feed primarily on crustaceans. Their diet is dominated by Acetes and penaeid prawns, which form the major component of their intake. Feeding intensity varies seasonally, with higher feeding activity observed during the pre-monsoon and post-monsoon periods. This indicates that their feeding patterns are not uniform throughout the year and show clear seasonal peaks associated with these periods.

Thryssa cuvierii and Thryssa mystax exhibit an extended spawning period. Peak spawning activity occurs between November and March. Both species reach maturity at relatively small sizes. The size at first maturity is around 148 mm for males and 149 mm for females in Thryssa cuvierii, and around 135 mm for males and 136 mm for females in Thryssa mystax. This indicates that both species are capable of reproducing at smaller body sizes within their life cycle.

Anchovies such as Thryssa cuvierii and Thryssa mystax are exploited using ring seines, gill nets, and purse seines. Peak catches are observed from October to December. Among the two species, Thryssa mystax dominates the annual catch. The fishery shows clear seasonal variation, with higher catch rates concentrated within this period.

Seriolina nigrofasciata (Black-banded Trevally) is a carnivorous marine fish found in the Arabian Sea off the Karnataka coast. It inhabits offshore reefs on rocky substrates over the continental shelf at depths of 20 to 150 meters. It is a solitary species distributed across the Indian Ocean and western Pacific Ocean. Along the Karnataka coast it is captured using trawl nets, multi-day gillnets, outboard gillnets, and hook-and-line gear. It forages primarily in midwater habitats rather than along the seabed, as evidenced by the complete absence of benthic organisms and detritus in stomach contents.

Black-banded Trevally feeds predominantly on teleost fishes, particularly Nemipterus and Decapterus species, followed by cephalopods mainly squids, and to a lesser extent crustaceans such as shrimps and crabs. Smaller individuals consume a greater proportion of crustaceans while larger specimens shift toward fish-dominated diets. Peak feeding intensity occurs during April and May, with reduced feeding observed during November and December which corresponds to the spawning season. The reduction in feeding during spawning is attributed to fully developed gonads occupying abdominal cavity space and limiting stomach capacity.

Black-banded Trevally shows reduced feeding activity during its peak spawning season in November and December, consistent with reproductive investment patterns observed in other carangid species. Feeding intensity recovers after spawning as fish transition from reproductive to active foraging phases. This seasonal feeding and spawning cycle makes the species more available and catchable during April and May when post-spawning feeding peaks, and less active in late monsoon and early winter months.

Karnataka coastal waters observe an annual trawl fishing ban during June and July to protect spawning fish stocks during the monsoon season. During this period trawl net fishing is prohibited along the Karnataka coast in the Arabian Sea. Hook-and-line and some traditional gear methods may still operate depending on local regulations.

Panulirus homarus (Scalloped Spiny Lobster) demonstrates a broad salinity tolerance making it a resilient candidate for coastal Arabian Sea waters. Under gradual salinity shifts the species shows complete survival between 17 and 44 parts per thousand, while under sudden salinity changes survival is maintained between 26 and 41 parts per thousand. Beyond these ranges feeding activity stops completely and survival is compromised. This salinity tolerance information is critical for identifying suitable lobster fishing and aquaculture zones, particularly in the Bay of Bengal where monsoon-driven freshwater influx can cause significant salinity drops in coastal waters.

Coastal aquaculture and sea cage farming along the northwest Arabian Sea Saurashtra coast causes localized increases in nitrate and phosphate levels near farming sites. This nutrient enrichment leads to reduced phytoplankton diversity and increased dominance of pollution-tolerant algal species in surrounding waters. Elevated nutrient discharge from mariculture operations can trigger localized chlorophyll blooms and alter the natural phytoplankton community structure. Monitoring chlorophyll concentrations in coastal Arabian Sea waters can serve as an early indicator of nutrient loading from nearby aquaculture activity.

The Indian monsoon season provides a natural fallow period for coastal mariculture operations along the Arabian Sea. This monsoon-driven break from farming activity allows coastal ecosystems to recover from nutrient enrichment, supporting long-term marine ecosystem resilience. The seasonal pattern of farming activity followed by monsoon recovery is a climate-adaptive feature unique to Indian coastal waters and influences the annual cycle of phytoplankton productivity and water quality in Arabian Sea coastal zones.

Harmful algal blooms caused by the dinoflagellate Prorocentrum rhathymum occur in the coastal waters of the southwest Arabian Sea, particularly along the Kerala coast during August. These blooms are triggered by coastal upwelling which brings nutrient-rich water, specifically elevated ammonium and phosphate, to the surface. During bloom conditions chlorophyll concentrations can spike dramatically in nearshore surface waters, far exceeding typical open-ocean values. Such bloom events significantly alter the composition of phytoplankton communities, shifting from normal diatom-dominated assemblages to flagellate-dominated ones.

Coastal upwelling along the southwest Arabian Sea during the monsoon season drives harmful algal bloom formation by enriching surface waters with nutrients from deeper layers. Blooms typically peak in August and decline naturally by September as microzooplankton graze on the bloom organisms and environmental conditions shift. These bloom and post-bloom cycles cause measurable changes in surface chlorophyll levels and can negatively impact local fisheries by depleting oxygen and altering the food web structure that fish depend on.

Indian anchovy or scientifically known as Stolephorus indicus inhabits marine and brackish waters in pelagic-neritic environments along coastal regions of the northern Indian Ocean. It occurs within a depth range of 20–50 meters and is adapted to tropical water conditions. The species is widely distributed from the Red Sea to the coasts of Thailand, covering FAO Areas 51 and 57. Its range includes the Arabian Sea, Persian Gulf, Bay of Bengal, east African coast, Sri Lanka, Pakistan, Myanmar, and Thailand. It is consistently present along extensive stretches of the northern Indian Ocean coastline within these coastal environments.

Indian anchovy is an obligate schooling species that forms dense aggregations in surface and mid-water layers, particularly near the coast during productive seasons. These schools can be detected by surface disturbance when large groups are present. It feeds as a filter and particulate feeder on both phytoplankton and zooplankton in the surface and mid-water layer. Its diet includes diatoms, dinoflagellates, copepods, chaetognaths, and small invertebrate larvae. The species exhibits nocturnal vertical migration, rising to the surface at night and retreating to deeper waters during the day. During daylight hours, it remains within depths of 20–50 meters, while nighttime behavior brings it closer to the surface, where it is often targeted using light-luring methods.

Indian anchovy shows strong seasonal fishing patterns in the Arabian Sea. From January to May, it is a lower season with fish remaining offshore or dispersed, reducing coastal availability. From June to September, the southwest monsoon drives upwelling along the west coast, increasing productivity and forming anchovy schools associated with chlorophyll blooms. Peak fishing occurs from July to November as upwelling nutrients sustain productivity and post-monsoon schools move inshore and become accessible. From December to February, fishing remains moderate as the northeast monsoon continues and the season declines. Indian anchovy is one of the most abundant bait and food fish in Indian coastal waters year-round, with continuous presence but clear seasonal peaks tied to monsoon-driven productivity.

Indian anchovy shows strong seasonal fishing patterns in the Bay of Bengal. From January to May, availability is low to moderate with reduced accessibility. From June to September, monsoon conditions maintain moderate availability, but coastal fishing is limited by weather. Peak fishing occurs during October and November with the onset of the northeast monsoon, when fish schools concentrate inshore. From December to February, fishing remains good with schools accessible along the coast. Indian anchovy is one of the most abundant bait and food fish in Indian coastal waters year-round, with continuous presence but clear seasonal peaks tied to monsoon-driven productivity.

Indian anchovy is widely used as both a bait fish and a food fish across coastal regions. It is commonly processed in dried and salted form and traded in coastal markets throughout South Asia and Southeast Asia. The species is also utilized for fish meal and fish oil production, contributing to various commercial industries. In addition to direct human consumption and processing uses, Indian anchovy plays an important role in supporting larger pelagic fisheries, serving as a prey species for commercially important fish such as tuna and seer fish.

behavior: prey species for larger pelagic fish

document: Hilsa or scientifically known as Tenualosa ilisha occupies a wide range of habitats across its anadromous life cycle, transitioning between marine, brackish, and freshwater environments with distinct depth and salinity ranges at each stage. Larvae and post-larvae are found in coastal planktonic offshore waters under marine conditions at depths of 0–50 meters. Juveniles inhabit estuaries, brackish lagoons, and lower river reaches in salinity ranging from 10–30 ppt, typically at depths of 0–20 meters. Sub-adults move into coastal marine foreshore areas and bays under marine salinity conditions of 30–35 ppt, occupying depths of 0–100 meters. Adult hilsa in the marine phase occur in coastal and offshore waters at depths of 0–200 meters under marine salinity. During spawning migration, adults enter freshwater rivers, lower to mid river reaches, and tidal zones in salinity conditions of 0–10 ppt, remaining near the river surface. The species is found in the foreshore areas, estuaries, brackish-water lakes, and freshwater rivers of the western division of the Indo-Pacific faunistic region. Its marine distribution extends from Iran and Iraq in the Persian Gulf, to the west coast of India in the Arabian Sea, and across the Bay of Bengal. Pelagic-neritic zone of the Bay of Bengal and adjacent coastal waters. The BoB provides the bulk of its marine foraging habitat, with ~72% of global hilsa catch taken from marine/coastal waters. Hilsa moves into and out of deltaic ecosystems depending on foraging density and water flow patterns. It acquires energy reserves in offshore waters until reaching maturity, then initiates upstream spawning migration.

Hilsa is a classic anadromous fish that spawns in freshwater rivers and grows and matures in the sea, migrating from the sea to rivers against the current. Key rivers for spawning migration include the Ganges/Hooghly in India, where migration can reach up to 1,200 km, the Brahmaputra, Meghna, Irrawaddy, Padma, and east coast Indian rivers such as the Godavari, Mahanadi, and Krishna. The Meghna river is the most important, contributing about 68% of inland hilsa production. Typical upstream migration distances range from 50–100 km, with maximum reported distances exceeding 1,200 km in the Ganges system. In some rivers, migration is restricted by barrages such as the Farakka Barrage on the Ganges. This migration pattern defines the species' life cycle, linking marine and freshwater systems across large geographic scales.

Hilsa forms large schools in coastal and estuarine waters, with schooling behavior especially prominent during monsoon and pre-monsoon seasons. These schools migrate through the surface and mid-water column, reflecting coordinated group movement across habitats. The species is also known to be a fast swimmer, with documented movement covering 71 km in a single day.

Juvenile hilsa, known as jatka, measuring 9–10 inches, appear in the foreshore of the Bay of Bengal during October–November each year. They remain for a short period in lower river reaches where they feed on rich coastal plankton, before migrating back to the sea to grow and later return as adults. Jatka form independent seasonal fisheries during March–April in eastern Indian coastal and riverine waters, reflecting their distinct life stage behavior and seasonal presence.

Hilsa is a trans-boundary shared stock distributed across multiple countries, with Bangladesh contributing approximately 76% of the global catch, followed by Myanmar at around 15% and India at about 4%. The remaining 5% of the catch comes from countries including Iraq, Iran, Kuwait, Thailand, and Pakistan. The total global annual catch approaches approximately 0.5 million tonnes, with Bangladesh alone contributing a major share. This distribution highlights the species' importance across regional fisheries and its shared nature across national boundaries, requiring coordinated management across countries within its distribution range.

Hilsa exhibits distinct seasonal patterns linked to spawning migration and juvenile movement across riverine and coastal systems. During January to February, jatka enter coastal and river waters, supporting a juvenile fishery, while some areas experience a secondary spawning season and adults overwinter in coastal waters. In March to April, large swarms of jatka enter Bengal estuaries for feeding and form an independent fishery, while pre-monsoon spawning migration begins for some populations during the January to March secondary season. From May to June, the main spawning migration begins as adults move from coastal marine waters to river mouths with the onset of the southwest monsoon, triggered by increasing river flows. Peak spawning occurs from June to September, when adults migrate far upstream and breed mainly in rivers, including tidal zones and deeper river reaches, with fish not feeding during river migration and peak river catches observed in August to September.

Hilsa shows post-spawning and coastal redistribution patterns from September to December, followed by strong coastal fishery activity. During September to October, post-spawning spent fish descend rivers, while juveniles and spent adults move toward coastal waters. Fishing restrictions are recommended near the Meghna estuary during spawning aggregations, particularly around new and full moon periods. Coastal foreshore fishing begins to recover during this period. From November to December, adult hilsa return to marine coastal waters and a new cohort of jatka, measuring 9–10 inches, appears. Feeding activity intensifies during this time, and marine catches begin to increase.

In the Bay of Bengal, marine catch remains dominant at approximately 72% of total production, with coastal waters sustaining a year-round hilsa fishery. Seasonal peaks occur during June to October for river migration and from October to February for coastal fishing activity.

Hilsa distribution, abundance, and migration are strongly influenced by oceanographic conditions in the Bay of Bengal. Primary productivity is a key driver of marine foraging and growth, with decadal observations showing a significant correlation between net primary productivity and hilsa catch volumes. Catches are closely linked to the availability of phytoplankton, zooplankton, and nutrients such as nitrate, phosphate, and silicate. The most productive areas of the Bay of Bengal, where net primary productivity exceeds 2,000 mg C/m²/day, span approximately 131,000 to 213,000 km² and correspond to major hilsa fishing grounds. River flow acts as a key trigger for spawning migration, with increased freshwater discharge during the southwest monsoon drawing hilsa upstream into rivers. Salinity gradients serve as navigational cues during this movement. The onset of the monsoon in June marks the start of upstream migration, while monsoon retreat in October corresponds to post-spawning return to coastal waters.

Hilsa is harvested using a variety of fishing methods across marine, coastal, and estuarine environments. The primary gear is the drift gillnet, locally known as chandi jal, with mesh sizes ranging from 65–125 mm. A standard gillnet unit consists of 100 × 200 meshes per piece, with 200–400 pieces operated per boat, using plastic floats and brick sinkers. Other gears include clapnets such as shanla jal or kona, which are used seasonally, as well as ring seines and purse seines in offshore marine fisheries. Set bag nets are commonly used in estuarine and coastal areas, and handlines with small trash fish bait are also employed. Fishing restrictions are recommended during September to October in lower Meghna spawning areas, particularly 3 days before and after new and full moon periods, to protect brood fish during spawning aggregations.

Hilsa is classified as Least Concern (LC) under IUCN and is recognized as a shared stock under FAO terminology, distributed across multiple countries. The Bay of Bengal Large Marine Ecosystem programme coordinates trans-boundary management of this resource. Marine yields have increased substantially over the last two decades, while river yields have remained relatively stable, indicating a shift from riverine to marine stock since the 1990s. Key threats to hilsa populations include river barrages such as the Farakka Barrage that obstruct migration routes, overharvesting of juvenile and brood fish, and habitat degradation in river delta systems. These factors influence stock sustainability and require coordinated management across regions.

Indian mackerel or scientifically known as Rastrelliger kanagurta inhabits marine pelagic-neritic environments and is an oceanodromous species found across the Indo-West Pacific region, including FAO Areas 51 and 57. Its distribution extends from the Red Sea and East Africa to Indonesia, northward to the Ryukyu Islands and China, and southward to Australia, Melanesia, and Samoa, with additional occurrence in the Mediterranean via the Suez Canal. The species occupies a depth range of 20–90 meters and is found in water temperatures ranging from 17–30°C, with a preferred temperature of around 27°C. It is a coastal pelagic species associated with continental shelves and island slopes, commonly occurring in coastal bays, harbours, and deep lagoons. Indian mackerel is typically found in turbid, plankton-rich waters and is strongly associated with upwelling zones and high-chlorophyll coastal regions. It remains within neritic inner shelf waters and does not venture into deep oceanic environments. Depth range reflects daytime habitat; vertical migrations into shallower layers at night.

Indian mackerel shows strong seasonal patterns along the southwest coast of India driven by monsoon cycles and spawning activity. During January to February, it is a low season under the northeast monsoon, with fish dispersed offshore and limited inshore availability. In March to April, pre-monsoon conditions bring fish inshore and spawning begins in some populations. By May, peak spawning season begins as mackerel move inshore ahead of the southwest monsoon. From June to July, the onset of the southwest monsoon triggers upwelling along the Kerala and Karnataka coast, marking a major spawning period, with catch levels building. Large adult mackerel are observed from mid-August onwards in offshore areas 6–20 miles from the coast. During August to September, post-spawning adults are more numerous along the northern southwest coast, and the fishery reaches its peak. From October to November, post-monsoon conditions support high inshore availability and major landings, with young fish entering the coastal fishery. November to December marks the peak season on the west coast, supported by cooler surface waters after upwelling. Peak landings occur from October to January on the west coast.
On the east coast of India in the Bay of Bengal, Indian mackerel shows secondary seasonal peaks during the northeast monsoon, with variability in catch linked to upwelling intensity.

Indian mackerel is an obligate schooling species that forms large, dense schools, often detected near the surface during feeding periods, with small groups also observed feeding on fish eggs immediately after spawning events. It exhibits a mixed feeding strategy, functioning as both a filter feeder and a particulate feeder, using gill rakers to filter phytoplankton such as diatoms while also actively feeding on small zooplankton. Its primary diet includes phytoplankton, cladocerans, ostracods, and larval polychaetes, along with secondary food sources such as macroplankton including larval shrimps and fish. Gill raker count increases with size, from 105 rakers at 12.7 cm fork length to 160 rakers at 19 cm fork length. Feeding activity is closely linked to phytoplankton blooms, and fishing grounds coincide with high chlorophyll areas. The species is also strongly associated with upwelling along the southwest coast, particularly the Kerala and Karnataka coast, where catch levels are correlated with sea level at Cochin prior to monsoon onset. Its response to upwelling is indirect through zooplankton availability and shows no regular seasonal pattern.

Indian mackerel is one of the most important small pelagic species in FAO Areas 51 and 57. India is the dominant producer, particularly along the west coast including Kerala, Karnataka, Goa, and Maharashtra. The species is classified as Least Concern (LC) by IUCN. It is harvested using purse seine, ring seine, and drift gillnet as the primary fishing gears. Indian mackerel is marketed in multiple forms including fresh, frozen, canned, dried-salted, smoked, and also processed into fish sauce, with a very high price category. It contributes significantly to food security and supports artisanal livelihoods along the Indian coastline.

Pomfret or scientifically known as Pampus argenteus inhabits marine benthopelagic environments and is an oceanodromous species found across the Indo-West Pacific, from the Persian Gulf to Indonesia and north to Japan, within FAO Areas 51 and 57, and is not recorded from Australasia. It occurs within a depth range of 5–110 m, typically over muddy and sandy bottoms, under subtropical temperature conditions with a preferred range of 21.9–29.1°C. It is an inshore benthopelagic species found over sandy and muddy bottoms at depths of 5–80 m and forms schools over soft-bottom continental shelf areas. The species associates with demersal species such as Nemipterus and Leiognathus in soft-bottom communities. Young juveniles ascend estuaries, including regions such as the Sundarbans and Khulna in Bangladesh and Balasore in Odisha. It remains restricted to the continental shelf and does not venture into deep oceanic waters.

Pomfret forms dense schools over muddy and sandy bottom habitats within the coastal-shelf benthopelagic zone. Adults feed primarily on gelatinous zooplankton including ctenophores, salps, and medusae, along with other zooplankton. A Kuwait study reported that the diet is dominated by crustaceans, with copepods comprising 39% and other crustaceans 16%, followed by Bacillariophyta at 21%, molluscs at 11%, and fish scales and eggs at 13%. The proportion of copepods in the diet increases with fish size. The species shows regional movement patterns, with Gulf populations migrating within the northern Gulf between Kuwait and Iran as a single stock unit. On the Indian shelf, populations exhibit seasonal inshore-offshore movement and do not undertake long-distance ocean migrations. Pomfret lacks pelvic fins entirely, which is a diagnostic characteristic.

Pomfret shows distinct seasonal patterns in the Arabian Sea and Persian Gulf with variations in availability, spawning, and fishing activity. From January to April, it is mostly offshore with limited catches during the winter season, occurring in deeper benthopelagic waters. From April to June, spawning begins, with peak spawning during this period for Indian Ocean populations. During June to September, the southwest monsoon brings rough seas, limiting trawl access while fish remain on the shelf bottom. From October to December, post-monsoon conditions improve fishing, with active trawl and gillnet seasons, fish accessible inshore, and high market demand and price. In the Gulf, pomfret contributes significantly to fisheries value, with historical declines in catch leading to fishing bans for stock recovery.

Pomfret shows seasonal patterns in the Bay of Bengal with variations in availability and fishing activity. From January to April, fish are mostly offshore with limited catches in deeper benthopelagic waters. From April to June, this period corresponds to pre-spawning movement with fish available inshore on the continental shelf. During June to September, monsoon conditions restrict fishing access while fish remain on the shelf bottom. From October to December, post-monsoon conditions support artisanal fishing, with fish available inshore and marketed locally.

Prawns or scientifically known as Penaeus monodon Fabricius occupy different habitats across life stages with distinct salinity and depth ranges. Nauplii and larvae occur in offshore marine pelagic waters at depths of 0–50 m under salinity conditions of 33–35 ppt. Post-larvae move into shallow coastal and inshore areas with low salinity ranging from 5–20 ppt at depths of 0–5 m. Juveniles inhabit estuaries, mangroves, and lagoons in brackish conditions of 2–25 ppt at very shallow depths of 0–2 m. Sub-adults migrate to offshore shelf areas under marine-brackish salinity conditions of 20–30 ppt at depths of 20–60 m. Adults during the breeding phase are found over soft-bottom shelf habitats such as muddy and sandy areas under marine salinity of 28–34 ppt at depths of 20–110 m. The mangrove-estuarine connection is critical for population maintenance, as juveniles and sub-adults spend 3–6 months in estuaries and mangroves as nursery grounds, and loss of mangroves directly leads to decline in prawn recruitment and coastal fishery yields.

Prawns exhibit distinct behavioral patterns across spawning, migration, feeding, and mating. Spawning occurs only in deep offshore marine waters, where eggs are spherical and yellowish-green with a diameter of 0.27–0.31 mm, and all larval stages from nauplii to protozoea to mysis are pelagic and offshore. Post-larvae migrate inshore into estuaries and mangroves as nursery habitats, showing high tolerance to salinity and temperature fluctuations, while sub-adults begin offshore migration after 3–6 months. Adults remain offshore on soft-bottom shelf habitats at depths of 20–110 m for spawning. The species is nocturnal, burrowing into the substrate during the day and emerging at night to feed. Feeding behavior is primarily predatory under natural conditions, with active benthic feeding at night on organic detritus, benthic algae, small invertebrates, worms, molluscs, and other crustaceans. Mating is also nocturnal, with the male depositing a spermatophore in the female's thelycum just after moulting during the soft-shell stage, and females mature from approximately 70 g body weight.

Prawns in the Arabian Sea show distinct seasonal patterns linked to monsoon cycles. From January to February, there are some trawl catches under northeast monsoon conditions with good offshore fishing. From March to May, the pre-monsoon period involves trawlers actively targeting adults on the offshore shelf, marking the peak pre-monsoon trawl season. From June to September, the southwest monsoon brings Marine Fishing Ban conditions, with trawling restricted and a typical 61-day ban on the west coast (June 1–July 31). During this period, post-larval inshore recruitment peaks in mangrove nurseries. From October to February, the post-monsoon period represents the peak trawling season with the best catches, as juveniles grow to market size and both artisanal and commercial fisheries are active. Prawns are a primary beneficiary of the Marine Fishing Ban, which protects spawning adults and supports juvenile recruitment.

Prawns in the Bay of Bengal show seasonal patterns linked to monsoon cycles. From January to February, this is a post-monsoon trawl season with good offshore catches. From March to May, it remains a good trawl season with fish accessible before the monsoon. From June to September, monsoon conditions bring Marine Fishing Ban restrictions, with a typical 45-day ban on the east coast (April 15–May 29), and larval recruitment peaks in estuaries and delta systems. From October to February, the post-monsoon period is the peak trawling season with the best catches, as juveniles grow to market size and both artisanal and commercial fisheries are active. Prawns benefit from the Marine Fishing Ban, which protects spawning adults and supports juvenile recruitment.

Prawns have significant commercial importance in both aquaculture and wild capture fisheries across the Indian Ocean region. In aquaculture, they are the second most cultured shrimp globally, with production of approximately 770,000 t in 2009 valued at around US$3.65 billion. Wild capture fisheries are also important, particularly in countries such as India, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, and Indonesia. The species is affected by several major disease threats, including White Spot Syndrome Virus (WSSV), which is the most devastating, along with Yellowhead Virus and Monodon Baculovirus (MBV). For aquaculture, optimal culture conditions recommended by FAO include temperatures of 28–33°C and salinity levels of 15–25 ppt.

Sardine or scientifically known as Sardinella longiceps Valenciennes inhabits marine pelagic-neritic environments and is an oceanodromous species found only in the northern and western Indian Ocean. Its distribution includes the Gulf of Aden, Gulf of Oman, southwest India along Kerala, Karnataka, and Goa, and extends along the east coast of India up to Andhra Pradesh, but it is not found in the Red Sea or Persian Gulf. Historically, it was concentrated between 8°N and 14°N along the Malabar coast, but since around 1985 its range has expanded northward to approximately 20°N. It occurs from the surface to depths of 200 m, typically within 20–100 m, under tropical temperature conditions. The species is a coastal pelagic, continental shelf species strongly tied to upwelling zones and is found exclusively in areas of elevated surface productivity. Its distribution is directly controlled by sea surface temperature and upwelling intensity, and it forms dense surface schools in neritic waters.

Sardine forms extremely dense schools in the surface layer, often detectable by surface boiling and color changes in the water. It is a direct phytoplankton filter feeder, unlike mackerel, with primary food consisting of large diatoms such as Fragilaria oceanica, whose high abundance serves as a reliable indicator of sardine abundance and a predictor of strong year classes. It also feeds on dinoflagellates and copepods. Among pelagic fish on the Indian coast, sardine has the strongest direct link to upwelling intensity. The success of the sardine fishery is statistically related to sea level at Cochin just before monsoon onset, which acts as a remote forcing indicator of upwelling. The relationship follows a sequence where sea level influences remote forcing of upwelling, which in turn affects recruitment success rather than wind-driven upwelling itself. Strong remote forcing can inhibit recruitment by excluding spawning fish from the neritic zone due to oxygen-deficient upwelled water. Upwelling in the southeast Arabian Sea produces larger phytoplankton such as diatoms compared to the northeast Arabian Sea, supporting sardine populations that feed directly on phytoplankton.

Sardine shows clear seasonal patterns along the southwest coast driven by monsoon cycles and upwelling. From January to March, it is a low season with fish dispersed or absent near the coast, while winter upwelling provides some nutrient supply and pre-spawning aggregation begins offshore. During April to May, in the pre-monsoon period, fish begin moving inshore, with sea surface temperature still warm and limited coastal availability. From June to September, the southwest monsoon initiates upwelling from June along the Kerala and Karnataka coast, leading to the formation of sardine schools with chlorophyll blooms, marking the start of the main fishing season as upwelling intensifies, with peak sardine biomass usually during September to October. From October to December, the post-monsoon period represents the peak fishing season on the southwest coast, with upwelling nutrients still present in the water column, massive schools accessible inshore, and peak landings typically from October to January. From January to February, the season tapers off as fish move offshore or northward with warming sea surface temperature. Over the long term, sardine distribution has expanded northward beyond 14°N to significant quantities north of 20°N, linked to warming trends of +0.6°C over 1971–2020 on the southwest Indian coast.

Seer fish or scientifically known as Scomberomorus commerson inhabits marine pelagic-neritic environments and is an oceanodromous species distributed across the Indo-West Pacific, from the Red Sea and East Africa to Southeast Asia, north to Japan, and south to southeast Australia and Fiji, and is also a Lessepsian migrant in the Mediterranean via the Suez Canal, within FAO Areas 51 and 57. It occurs from the surface to approximately 70 m, with reports up to 200 m, under tropical warm surface water conditions ranging from 18–30°C. It is an inshore pelagic predator associated with continental shelf edges, drop-offs, and sloping reefs, and is also found in shallow coastal waters, estuaries, and lagoons where it tolerates turbidity and low salinity. The species hunts in specific micro-habitats including reef edges, drop-offs, current-swept headlands, and river mouths, and does not form large open-ocean aggregations like tuna, remaining more closely associated with reef and coastal environments.

Seer fish is a highly active visual predator with large compressed knife-like teeth suited for slashing prey, often attacking schools of baitfish such as anchovies, sardines, and mackerel from below or from the side. It is typically solitary or found in small groups, unlike schooling tuna, and functions as an ambush predator at reef edges and current-swept points. Its diet includes anchovies, sardines, Indian mackerel, penaeid shrimps, and squid or other cephalopods, and is opportunistic, varying by season. As a top predator, its catches track the abundance of prey species, especially small pelagics that follow upwelling zones. Some populations undertake long shore-side migrations while others remain permanent residents of specific reef areas. The species is a batch spawner near reef slopes and spawning aggregation sites, with documented spawning aggregations in specific areas, and exhibits rapid early growth, reaching commercially important size of 60 cm fork length within 1–2 years.

Seer fish shows clear seasonal patterns in the Arabian Sea with variations in availability and fishing conditions. From January to February, this is one of the best fishing seasons during the northeast monsoon with calm seas, fish accessible and concentrated near the surface, marking an artisanal peak season. From March to May, fishing continues during the pre-monsoon with fish moving along the coast. From June to September, the southwest monsoon brings rough seas reducing fishing activity, with offshore fish less accessible and larger fish remaining below the surface. From October to November, post-monsoon conditions bring fish back inshore with one of the better fishing seasons along the Karnataka, Goa, and Maharashtra coast. From December to January, fishing remains good with fish concentrated inshore during the northeast monsoon.

behavior: offshore and deeper movement during monsoon

document: Seer fish shows seasonal patterns in the Bay of Bengal with variations in availability and fishing conditions. From January to February, this is a good fishing period with fish inshore and accessible. From March to May, it remains a moderate fishing period with fish active. From June to September, monsoon conditions limit fishing and fish move offshore. From October to November, this is the peak fishing season with Surmai or Vanjaram fishing peaking and major landings recorded. From December to January, fishing remains good during the northeast monsoon with fish concentrated inshore.

Seer fish is classified as Near Threatened (NT) under the IUCN status A2bd assessment conducted in 2022. Catches have been declining in several parts of the Indian Ocean, indicating pressure on the stock. The species is vulnerable to overfishing due to slow maturation, reaching first maturity at approximately 75 cm and around 2 years of age. There are no specific management measures under the Indian Ocean Tuna Commission for seer fish, and the species is managed only at the national level.

Skipjack tuna or scientifically known as Katsuwonus pelamis inhabits marine pelagic-oceanic environments and is an oceanodromous species with a cosmopolitan distribution in tropical and warm-temperate waters, not found in the Black Sea, and occurring in FAO Areas 51 and 57 of the Indian Ocean. It occupies depths from 0–260 m, primarily within 0–100 m, under temperature conditions ranging from 14.7–30°C with a preferred temperature of 27°C. It is primarily a surface-schooling species within 0–100 m, with larvae and juveniles restricted to surface waters of 15–30°C. Compared to yellowfin, it is less depth-restricted and lacks the same thermoregulatory capacity as large Thunnus species. Surface schools follow warm water masses and thermocline depth. It is found in open ocean offshore waters with a strong schooling tendency at the surface and commonly associates with floating objects, other species, seabirds, and convergence zones.

Skipjack tuna exhibits a strong tendency to school in surface waters, forming groups often associated with birds, drifting objects, sharks, and whales, and displaying characteristic behaviors such as jumping, feeding frenzies, and surface foaming, with multi-species schools common alongside yellowfin and juvenile bigeye. It is an omnivorous apex predator within its size class, feeding on fish, crustaceans, cephalopods, and mollusks, with cannibalism also common. The species is a highly active, continuous feeder with a high metabolic rate that requires near-constant food intake and closely follows surface chlorophyll blooms due to prey availability. It is considered a single Indian Ocean-wide stock with rapid and large-scale movements across the Indian Ocean and less inter-ocean exchange compared to yellowfin. Spawning occurs continuously throughout the year in tropical waters, with reproductively active females spawning almost daily, releasing eggs in multiple batches.

document: Skipjack tuna shows distinct seasonal patterns in the Arabian Sea influenced by monsoon cycles and sea surface temperature. From January to March, good surface fishing occurs under northeast monsoon conditions, with fish accessible off Maldives and Lakshadweep. From April to May, the pre-monsoon period provides optimal sea surface temperature with excellent surface schooling and peak pole-and-line fishing in the Maldives. From June to September, the southwest monsoon drives upwelling, pushing skipjack to the surface and creating some of the best purse seine fishing grounds in Maldives and Sri Lanka despite rough and cloudy conditions. From October to November, post-monsoon calmer seas support excellent schooling and good fishing conditions. From December to January, northeast monsoon conditions bring variable fishing availability.

Skipjack tuna shows seasonal patterns in the Bay of Bengal and eastern Indian Ocean influenced by sea surface temperature and monsoon cycles. From January to March, fishing is patchy with cooler sea surface temperatures but improving surface availability. From April to May, warming sea surface temperature leads to increasing schools approaching the coast. From June to September, monsoon conditions with choppy seas limit fishing and purse seining, with some offshore catch. From October to November, this period represents the best skipjack season with fish aggregating near the surface under calmer post-monsoon conditions. From December to January, fish move south toward the equatorial Indian Ocean, affecting local availability.

Skipjack tuna in the Indian Ocean is classified as maximally sustainably fished according to the IOTC stock status in 2022, meaning it is not overfished and not subject to overfishing. Annual catch in the Indian Ocean is approximately 350,000–400,000 t, while globally it is one of the most caught fish species with around 2.8 million t per year and ranked #3 by volume at 2,795,339 t in 2012. The species is primarily harvested using purse seine fisheries around FADs, with additional methods including pole-and-line or baitboat fisheries in India, Maldives, and Sri Lanka, as well as handlines, trolling, and driftnets. It is predominantly marketed as canned fish at less than US$1 per kg, while in Maldives it is also consumed fresh or smoked as traditional Maldive fish, and in India it is used for fresh and frozen local consumption. Skipjack tuna distribution and fishing success are linked to oceanographic conditions, following warm isotherms above 24°C, with surface chlorophyll blooms indicating prey concentration, thermocline depth controlling its lower vertical limit, and being less constrained by oxygen minimum zones compared to yellowfin due to its shallower habitat and reduced deep-diving behavior.

Squid or Uroteuthis duvaucelii inhabits marine pelagic-neritic and benthopelagic environments across the Indian Ocean, including the Red Sea and Arabian Sea, extending eastwards from Mozambique to the South China Sea and Philippine Sea, and north to Taiwan, within FAO Areas 51 and 57. It occurs at depths of 3–170 m, with highest abundance between 30–120 m, under tropical temperature conditions. It is an inshore neritic species associated with continental shelf habitats. The species performs diel vertical migrations, remaining near the seafloor during the day where it is targeted by demersal trawl fisheries, and ascending to the upper water column at night where it is targeted by light-lure and surface fisheries. Juveniles are found in shallower nearshore waters, while adults occupy deeper shelf waters.

Squid exhibits diel vertical migration behavior, aggregating near the seafloor during the day at depths of 30–120 m and ascending to the surface at night to feed, creating two distinct fishing opportunities of daytime bottom trawl and nighttime jigging or light luring. It spawns continuously throughout the year with two seasonal peaks in July and October in the Arabian Sea, producing two annual cohorts. Spawning involves benthic egg masses laid on the seafloor that hatch into planktonic paralarvae which migrate to surface waters, with recruitment to the trawl fishery occurring at approximately 2 months of age. The species has a very short life span of around 6 months and exhibits rapid growth, with average daily growth rates of 1.22 mm mantle length per day in females and 1.48 mm mantle length per day in males, with monsoon conditions strongly affecting early growth, as poor monsoon slows growth in the first 40 days post-hatch. Squid is an active carnivore feeding on mysids, euphausiids, ostracods, Acetes species, small fish, and other squid with cannibalism commonly documented, using tentacles to capture prey and a beak to deliver a lethal bite, supported by large eyes adapted for low-light hunting.

Squid (Uroteuthis duvaucelii) shows distinct seasonal patterns in the Arabian Sea with variations in trawl and jigging availability. From January to May, the pre-monsoon period supports good trawl catches with squid accessible on the shelf bottom. From June to September, the southwest monsoon restricts trawling due to weather and fishing bans, allowing squid populations to rebuild, with a spawning peak in July. From October to February, the post-monsoon period is the peak trawl and jigging season with calm seas, best catches, and a spawning peak in October, with major landings along Kerala, Karnataka, and Gujarat. A recommended closed season during October to November is advised to protect brood animals and egg masses during peak spawning, involving closure of the trawl fishery.

Squid (Uroteuthis duvaucelii) shows seasonal patterns in the Bay of Bengal with variations in trawl and jigging availability. From January to May, this is a moderate period with bottom trawl fishery active. From June to September, monsoon conditions restrict trawling, allowing populations to rebuild, with two cohort pulses occurring in July and October. From October to February, the post-monsoon period is the peak season with squid accessible, October spawning peak, and major landings. A recommended closed season during October to November is advised to protect brood animals and egg masses during peak spawning, involving closure of the trawl fishery.

Squid (Uroteuthis duvaucelii) has significant commercial importance in India, with approximately 97% of Indian squid landings consisting of this species. India is a major global exporter of squid, producing more than 80 types of processed squid products for international markets including Europe, particularly Spain and Italy, as well as Japan and China. Landings in Kerala alone increased from around 100 t in 1961 to approximately 35,000 t during the 2002–2016 decadal average period. Squid contributes substantially to India's foreign exchange earnings from marine fisheries.

Yellowfin tuna or Thunnus albacares inhabits marine pelagic-oceanic environments and is an oceanodromous species distributed worldwide in tropical and subtropical seas, absent from the Mediterranean, and present throughout the Indian Ocean in FAO Areas 51 and 57. It occurs across a depth range of 1–1,602 m, typically within 1–250 m, under temperature conditions of 15–31°C with a preferred temperature of 28°C. It primarily inhabits the epipelagic zone from 0–200 m and is constrained from below by the Oxygen Minimum Zone in the Arabian Sea, where it cannot tolerate oxygen levels below approximately 0.5 ml/L, compressing its habitat to around 0–250 m. It schools at the surface during daylight and makes deeper dives of approximately 400–1,000 m at night. The species possesses thermoregulatory adaptations with retia mirabilia that allow body temperature to exceed ambient conditions by several degrees, extending its vertical range. It is oceanic and rarely associated with reefs, schooling primarily by size either monospecifically or with other tuna and billfish, with large individuals frequently associating with porpoises and floating debris such as FADs.

Yellowfin tuna forms large, size-sorted schools at the surface and commonly associates with floating objects, Fish Aggregating Devices, whale sharks, and cetaceans, with surface schools targeted by purse seine fisheries. It occupies epipelagic waters during the day, primarily within 0–100 m, and makes occasional deep dives to 300–600 m, with a maximum reported depth of 1,602 m, with vertical habitat bounded below by the thermocline and Oxygen Minimum Zone. It is an apex predator and opportunistic feeder, consuming schooling prey available in the epipelagic zone including fish, squid, and crustaceans, functioning as an active visual predator with feeding primarily occurring above the thermocline. The species is highly migratory, with a single stock assumed per ocean basin based on RTTP-IO tagging, moving across ocean basins with large-scale mixing between the Indian Ocean and adjacent oceans appearing limited, and tagging studies showing complex movements with individuals traveling 1,000s of km.

document: Yellowfin tuna shows seasonal patterns in the Arabian Sea influenced by monsoon conditions and sea surface dynamics. From January to March, moderate catches occur under northeast monsoon winds with improving sea state and some longline activity. From April to May, the pre-monsoon season provides good fishing as fish are accessible ahead of the southwest monsoon. From June to September, the southwest monsoon drives upwelling, compressing tuna into the surface layer due to the Oxygen Minimum Zone below and thermocline above, creating peak purse seine fishing season off Maldives, Sri Lanka, and Lakshadweep. From October to November, post-monsoon calmer seas support good surface schooling off the west coast of India. From December to January, northeast monsoon conditions bring moderate fishing with active longline fisheries.

document: Yellowfin tuna shows seasonal patterns in the Bay of Bengal influenced by monsoon conditions and sea surface temperature. From January to March, catches are reduced as northeast monsoon conditions limit surface schooling. From April to May, warming sea surface temperature causes fish to move toward the surface under mixed conditions. From June to September, monsoon conditions disrupt surface fishing with patchy schooling, and some offshore longline catches occur. From October to November, this is the best surface fishing season with fish concentrated and high purse seine activity. From December to January, catches decline again with the onset of the northeast monsoon.

Yellowfin tuna in the Indian Ocean is classified as overfished and subject to overfishing according to the IOTC stock status in 2022, indicating a major concern for stock sustainability. Annual catch in the Indian Ocean is approximately 450,000–500,000 t, and globally it ranks #6 by catch volume with 1,352,204 t recorded in 2012. The species is primarily harvested using purse seine fisheries targeting small to medium fish, while longline fisheries target larger individuals for the sashimi market, particularly in Japan. Additional fishing methods include pole-and-line fisheries in India, Sri Lanka, and Maldives, as well as artisanal fisheries in India, Sri Lanka, Maldives, and Indonesia. Large yellowfin are sold in sashimi markets at US$5–15 per kg, while in India they are consumed fresh or frozen in regions such as Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and the Andaman Islands. Distribution and catchability are linked to oceanographic conditions, with fish following warm isotherms above 24°C, and thermocline depth defined by D20 and D26 acting as the primary vertical habitat constraint. In the Arabian Sea, the Oxygen Minimum Zone compresses fish into a thin surface layer, increasing surface catch rates compared to the open ocean, while sea surface temperature fronts, eddies, and convergence zones concentrate prey and are directly linked to potential fishing zone advisories.

document: Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) belongs to Class Teleostei, Order Clupeiformes (herrings), Family Engraulidae (anchovies), and Subfamily Engraulinae. It is classified as Least Concern (LC) under IUCN status, assessed in 2017. The species was previously synonymised with several other Stolephorus species. Hata et al. (2021) confirmed Stolephorus indicus as a valid species distinct from Stolephorus commersonnii, Stolephorus rex, Stolephorus balinensis, and Stolephorus scitulus. Older fisheries records may conflate these species. The species previously referred to as Stolephorus commersonnii is now recognized as endemic to Mauritius, while true Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) represents the key species across the Indian Ocean.

Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) inhabits marine and brackish pelagic-neritic environments and is an oceanodromous species occurring within tropical climate zones. It occupies a depth range of 20–50 m and forms dense schools in nearshore pelagic waters, often close to the surface. The species is distributed across latitudes 30°N–37°S and longitudes 23°E–144°W. It plays a critical ecological role as a key prey species for larger fish, seabirds, and marine mammals, functioning as an important intermediate in trophic food webs of the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal.

Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) reaches a maximum length of 15.5 cm standard length (SL) and a common length of around 12.0 cm. The length at first maturity is approximately 9.0 cm. It has a trophic level of around 2.2, functioning as a planktivore or low-level omnivore. The species exhibits high resilience due to its small size, fast growth, and short lifespan, and has low vulnerability to fishing pressure.

Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) is widely distributed across the Indian Ocean from the Red Sea to Thailand, including the Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal, east coast of Africa, Persian Gulf, and extending eastward to Myanmar and Thailand. Key regions include the entire Indian coastline on both west and east coasts, Sri Lanka, Pakistan, Oman, Gulf of Aden, and the Persian Gulf. It is one of the most abundant small pelagic species in Indian coastal waters. The species is also recorded as a Lessepsian migrant, first observed in the eastern Mediterranean near Tel Aviv, Israel in May 2015, having entered through the Suez Canal from the Red Sea.

Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) feeds as both a filter feeder and particulate feeder on phytoplankton and zooplankton in the upper water column. Its diet includes diatoms, dinoflagellates, copepods, chaetognaths, and small invertebrate larvae. Feeding occurs primarily near the surface, often associated with nocturnal vertical migration when the species ascends to upper layers to feed.

Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) is a short-lived species with a lifespan of less than one year in tropical waters. It spawns throughout the year with seasonal peaks. Larvae are pelagic and recruit rapidly into the population. The species exhibits fast growth typical of clupeiform fishes and supports high turnover fisheries.

Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) is a slender, elongate fish with a rounded cross-section and a rounded belly bearing 2–6 small needle-like pre-pelvic scutes. It has no dorsal or anal spines, with dorsal soft rays numbering 15–17 and anal soft rays 19–21. The maxilla tip is pointed, reaching to or just beyond the front border of the pre-operculum, whose hind border is convex and rounded. Lower gill rakers number 20–28. The isthmus muscle tapers evenly forward to the hind border of the branchial membrane. Pelvic fin tips do not extend below the dorsal fin origin, and the anal fin is short with its origin below the centre of the dorsal fin base. The body is pale, translucent fleshy brown with a silver mid-lateral stripe and lacks dark pigment lines on the back between the head and dorsal fin origin.

Indian Anchovy (Stolephorus indicus) is commercially important and widely used as both a bait fish and a food fish. It is commonly processed in dried and salted form and traded in coastal markets across South Asia and Southeast Asia. The species is also used for fish meal and fish oil production. It falls under a low to medium price category in fisheries markets.

Hilsa (Tenualosa ilisha) reaches a maximum length of 60.0 cm standard length, with common lengths around 36.0 cm standard length and 42.0 cm total length. It attains a maximum weight of approximately 2.5 kg and can live up to 5 years. Length at first maturity is around 31.3 cm. The species has a trophic level of approximately 2.9 and exhibits medium resilience, with a population doubling time of 1.4–4.4 years. Fishing vulnerability is low to moderate, while climate vulnerability is very high. Generation time is about 2 years. Preferred temperature ranges from 21.3–28.0°C with a mean of 24.7°C. Length-weight relationship parameters are a=0.00891 and b=3.05 based on total length.

Hilsa (Tenualosa ilisha) is distributed across the Indian Ocean from the Persian Gulf eastward to Myanmar, including both western and eastern coasts of India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and Myanmar. It is also reported from the Gulf of Tonkin in Vietnam and river systems such as the Tigris basin and rivers of southern Iran. The Bay of Bengal represents the most important region for this species, serving as the primary spawning and feeding ground.

Hilsa (Tenualosa ilisha) feeds primarily on plankton through filter feeding and also exhibits bottom grubbing behavior on muddy substrates. Its diet consists mainly of phytoplankton and zooplankton. Adults are predominantly planktivorous.

Hilsa (Tenualosa ilisha) breeds mainly in rivers, typically migrating upstream up to about 50 km, and in some cases over 1,000 km such as in the Ganges. The primary breeding season occurs during the southwest monsoon from June to September, with a secondary season from January to March. Younger individuals may breed in tidal river zones. Artificial propagation has been partially successful in India. The species is a strong swimmer capable of covering up to 71 km in a single day.

Hilsa (Tenualosa ilisha) has no dorsal or anal spines, with dorsal soft rays numbering 18–21 and anal soft rays 18–23. The belly bears 30–33 scutes. The upper jaw has a distinct median notch. Gill rakers are fine and numerous, approximately 100–250 on the lower arch. Fins are hyaline. A dark blotch is present behind the gill opening, followed by small spots along the flank in juveniles. In life, the body is silver with gold and purple iridescence.

Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta) belongs to Class Teleostei, Order Scombriformes (mackerels), Family Scombridae (mackerels, tunas, bonitos), and Subfamily Scombrinae. It is classified as Least Concern (LC) under IUCN status, assessed in March 2022.

Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta) inhabits marine pelagic-neritic environments and is an oceanodromous species. It occurs at depths of 20–90 m under tropical conditions with temperatures ranging from 17°C and a preferred temperature around 27°C. The species is distributed between latitudes 38°N–36°S and longitudes 20°E–180°E. Adults are commonly found in coastal bays, harbours, and deep lagoons, particularly in turbid, plankton-rich waters. It is a schooling species, and both eggs and larvae are pelagic.

Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta) reaches a maximum length of 36.0 cm total length in males/unsexed individuals and 42.1 cm in females, with a common length of about 25.0 cm. It attains a maximum weight of approximately 859 g and lives up to 4 years. Length at first maturity is around 21.0 cm. The species has a trophic level of about 3.3. It exhibits medium resilience with a population doubling time of 1.4–4.4 years, low fishing vulnerability, and moderate to high climate vulnerability. Generation time is approximately 1.1 years. Preferred temperature ranges from 23.7–28.3°C with a mean of 27.3°C. Length-weight relationship parameters are a=0.00871 and b=3.07 based on total length.

Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta) is distributed across the Indo-West Pacific from the Red Sea and East Africa to Indonesia, extending northward to the Ryukyu Islands and China, and southward to Australia, Melanesia, and Samoa. It has also entered the eastern Mediterranean Sea through the Suez Canal. Key regions for fisheries in the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal include both west and east coasts of India, Sri Lanka, Pakistan, Oman, the Gulf of Aden, and the Persian Gulf eastward to Indonesia.
