document: Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta) feeds on phytoplankton and small zooplankton including diatoms, cladocerans, ostracods, and larval polychaetes, and also consumes macroplankton such as larval shrimps and larval fish. Small groups have been observed feeding on fish eggs immediately after spawning events. It uses a filter feeding mechanism supported by numerous gill rakers, which increase in number with size, allowing efficient phytoplankton filtration.

Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta) has dorsal spines numbering 8–11 and dorsal soft rays 12, with no anal spines and 12 anal soft rays. The head is longer than body depth. The maxilla is partly concealed by the lachrymal bone but extends to about the hind margin of the eye. A distinct black spot is present near the lower margin of the pectoral fin. The interpelvic process is small and single, and a swim bladder is present with a rudimentary anal spine. The body is silvery with several dark stripes on the upper half, which break into spots posteriorly. The body is fully scaled, moderately compressed, with a large mouth and very small teeth.

Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta) is a highly commercial species marketed fresh, frozen, canned, dried-salted, smoked, and processed into fish sauce. It falls under a very high price category. Nutritional composition per 100 g wet weight includes approximately 20.6% protein, 0.32 g Omega-3 fatty acids, 200 mg calcium, 3.28 mg iron, 47.6 µg selenium, and 1.24 mg zinc.

Pomfret (Pampus argenteus) belongs to Class Teleostei, Order Scombriformes, and Family Stromateidae (butterfishes). Its IUCN status is Not Evaluated. Taxonomic clarification indicates that Pampus cinereus is a separate valid species, and records from different regions may refer to either species.

Pomfret (Pampus argenteus) inhabits marine benthopelagic environments and is an oceanodromous species. It occurs at depths of 5–110 m under subtropical conditions with preferred temperatures ranging from 21.9–29.1°C and a mean of 28.1°C. The species is distributed between latitudes 46°N–10°S and longitudes 47°E–142°E. It is an inshore coastal shelf species commonly found in schools over muddy bottoms and is associated with demersal fishes such as Nemipterus and Leiognathus. It is primarily a coastal shelf species.

Pomfret (Pampus argenteus) reaches a maximum length of 60.0 cm standard length with a common length of about 30.0 cm, and can live up to 7 years. Length at first maturity is around 25.3 cm. The species has a trophic level of approximately 3.3. It exhibits medium resilience with a population doubling time of 1.4–4.4 years, low to moderate fishing vulnerability, and moderate to high climate vulnerability. Generation time is about 2.5 years. Preferred temperature ranges from 21.9–29.1°C with a mean of 28.1°C. Length-weight relationship parameters are a=0.01820 and b=3.02 based on total length.

Pomfret distribution in the Indo-West Pacific extends from the Persian Gulf to Indonesia, north to Hokkaido, Japan, and is not recorded from Australasia. There are extralimital captures from the Adriatic and off Hawaii, and in the northeastern Atlantic 3 captures reported. Key regions for the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal include being highly important in the Persian Gulf, Arabian Sea, entire Indian coastline, Bay of Bengal, Sri Lanka, and the Gulf of Thailand. It is one of the highest-priced fish in Gulf markets.

Pomfret (Pampus argenteus) feeds primarily on gelatinous zooplankton, including ctenophores, salps, medusae, and other zooplankton groups. Adults are specialized feeders on gelatinous plankton. The species lacks pelvic fins, which influences its feeding manoeuvrability.

Pomfret (Pampus argenteus) spawns from late winter through summer with peak spawning between April and June. In the northern Indian Ocean, spawning is associated with pre-monsoon and monsoon periods. Eggs and larvae are pelagic.

Pomfret (Pampus argenteus) has no dorsal spines and 37–43 dorsal soft rays, with vertebrae numbering 34–37. The body is firm, very deep, oval, and strongly compressed. The operculum is absent and the gill opening is reduced to a vertical slit. The dorsal and anal fins are preceded by 5–10 blade-like spines. Pelvic fins are absent, which is a key diagnostic feature. The caudal fin is deeply forked with the lower lobe longer than the upper. The body is gray above and silvery white below with small black dots, and fins are faintly yellow with darker edges on vertical fins.

Pomfret human uses include being sold fresh in local markets or shipped frozen to urban centres and also used in Chinese traditional medicine. It is one of the highest-value fish in the Arabian Gulf and Indian markets. The price category is high. Nutrients per 100 g wet weight include calcium 30.2 mg [12.9–71.1], iron 0.423 mg [0.222–1.065], protein 18% [17–19], Omega-3 0.264 g [0.154–0.448], selenium 68.2 µg [22.3–198.9], and zinc 0.442 mg [0.263–0.770].
Prawns or shrimp (Penaeus monodon) belong to Kingdom Animalia, Phylum Arthropoda, Class Malacostraca, Order Decapoda, and Family Penaeidae.

Prawns (Penaeus monodon) inhabit marine and brackish benthic and demersal environments across tropical regions from East Africa and the Arabian Peninsula through Southeast Asia to northern Australia. They occur at depths from shallow estuarine waters to about 110 m offshore. Juveniles and sub-adults occupy estuaries, lagoons, and mangrove areas that serve as nursery grounds, while adults migrate offshore to muddy or sandy bottoms for maturation and breeding. The species prefers temperatures of 23–33°C and salinity of 2–34 ppt, showing optimal growth at 28–33°C and 15–25 ppt. It is eurythermal and euryhaline. Prawns are nocturnal, burrowing into the substrate during the day and emerging at night to feed. The mangrove-estuarine connection is critical, and loss of mangroves directly reduces recruitment and population abundance.

Prawns (Penaeus monodon) reach a maximum body length of about 336 mm, with females growing larger than males. Females typically measure 25–30 cm and weigh 200–320 g, while males measure 20–25 cm and weigh 100–170 g. The species has a lifespan of about 1.5–2 years, with females living slightly longer. Preferred temperature ranges from 24.7–29.1°C with a mean of 28.1°C. Broodstock females are typically 200–320 g and males 100–170 g, usually collected from offshore depths greater than 60–80 m. Males develop spermatozoa at around 35 g body weight, and females become gravid at about 70 g. Mating occurs at night shortly after moulting when the exoskeleton is soft.

Prawns/Shrimp distribution includes a native range in the Indo-Pacific from the eastern coast of Africa and the Arabian Peninsula, east through South and Southeast Asia, north to Japan and Korea, and south to Northern Australia and Papua New Guinea. Key regions for the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal include the entire Indian coastline (both coasts), Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Gulf of Bengal deltas, mangrove zones of the Sundarbans (Bay of Bengal), and Gulf of Kutch and Mannar (Arabian Sea). Estuarine nursery grounds are present along all major Indian river systems.

Prawns/Shrimp feeding includes a diet where under natural conditions P. monodon is more of a predator than an omnivorous scavenger, and adults are benthic feeders that emerge at night to search for food. Food items include organic detritus, benthic algae, small invertebrates, worms, molluscs, other crustaceans, and organic matter on muddy or sandy bottoms. A note states that after moulting, the new soft shell makes prawns temporarily vulnerable and they may be cannibalized by companions.

Prawns/Shrimp life cycle and mating behaviour includes life history phases where spawning occurs in deep offshore marine waters with eggs spherical and yellowish-green (0.27–0.31 mm dia) and development progressing 2-cell to 4-cell to morula to embryo in approximately 11 hrs. Larval stages include nauplii to protozoea to mysis (all pelagic) in offshore marine waters. Post-larvae and juveniles move inshore into estuaries, lagoons, and mangrove zones as nursery grounds and are highly tolerant of salinity and temperature fluctuations. Sub-adults begin offshore migration. Adults are fully offshore in 20–110 m depth on muddy or sandy bottoms. Mating is nocturnal just after the female moults, with the male depositing a spermatophore in the female's thelycum. Seasonality in Indian waters shows peak spawning linked to pre-monsoon and monsoon seasons, with recruitment to coastal fisheries following after larval migration inshore.

Prawns/Shrimp morphological description includes a rostrum that is sigmoid, usually 7 dorsal and 3 ventral teeth, extending beyond the tip of the antennular peduncle, and a telson that is unarmed. The colour shows the carapace and abdomen transversely banded with alternating red and white, with antennae greyish brown, and pereiopods and pleopods brown with red fringing setae. In pond or brackish conditions, the colour shifts to dark brown. A distinctive feature is the large size and alternating dark stripes on the tail (tiger pattern) visible when fresh.

Prawns/Shrimp human uses include fisheries as highly commercial and aquaculture as a major farmed species worldwide, being the 2nd most cultured prawn globally (after Litopenaeus vannamei / whiteleg shrimp). In 2009 production was approximately 770,000 tonnes, valued at approximately USD 3.65 billion. Wild capture fisheries are important across the Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal, and surrounding coasts. It is prized for large size and sweet flavour and is extensively farmed in earthen ponds under extensive, semi-intensive, and intensive systems. Optimal aquaculture culture conditions are 28–33°C and salinity 15–25 ppt. Major disease threats in aquaculture include White Spot Syndrome Virus (WSSV), Yellowhead Virus, and Monodon Baculovirus (MBV). The price category is very high.
Sardinella longiceps (Indian oil sardine) belongs to Class Teleostei, Order Clupeiformes, and Family Dorosomatidae. Its IUCN status is Least Concern.

Sardinella longiceps inhabits marine pelagic-neritic environments in tropical coastal waters, typically at depths of 20–200 m. It forms dense schools in the upper water column and is strongly associated with upwelling zones, particularly along the southwest Indian coast (Malabar region, 8–14°N). Its distribution is restricted to the northern and western Indian Ocean, including the Arabian Sea, Gulf of Aden, Gulf of Oman, and extending along the Indian coast to Andhra Pradesh, possibly the Andaman region. It is absent from the Red Sea and Persian Gulf. The species plays an important ecological role as a planktivorous intermediate in the trophic food web.

Indian oil sardine biology includes a maximum length of 23.0 cm SL (published: up to 28 cm) and a common length of 20.0 cm SL. The maturity (Lm) is 16.3 cm. The trophic level is approximately 2.2 (planktivore). Resilience is high (fast-growing, short-lived), fishing vulnerability is low, and climate vulnerability is high with strong linkage to SST and upwelling conditions. A key ecology–climate link shows that it was historically distributed between 8–14°N (Malabar zone), and since approximately 1985 catches have been increasing north of 14°N as Indian Ocean surface temperatures warm.

Indian oil sardine distribution in the Indian Ocean is restricted to the northern and western parts only, including the Gulf of Aden, Gulf of Oman, southwest coast of India, and east coast of India to Andhra Pradesh. The highest concentrations occur along the southwest Indian coast (Kerala, Karnataka, Goa) during and after SW monsoon upwelling. It contributes approximately 10–15% of total marine fish catch in India.

Indian oil sardine feeding includes a diet as a planktivore that feeds by filtering phytoplankton (diatoms, dinoflagellates) and zooplankton (copepods). Food items include diatoms (including Fragilaria oceanica), dinoflagellates, and copepods. A high abundance of the diatom Fragilaria oceanica is an indicator of high oil sardine population in coastal waters. Feeding is directly linked to chlorophyll blooms and upwelling.

Sardinella longiceps exhibits oceanodromous movement with cyclical migrations between spawning and feeding areas over distances exceeding 100 km. Spawning occurs at night and is linked to monsoon seasonality. In Indian waters, peak spawning occurs post-monsoon from October to January, while in Omani waters peaks are observed in June–July and October. Length at 50% maturity is approximately 16.35 cm.

Indian oil sardine life cycle and mating behaviour includes spawning usually taking place at night and being oceanodromous, migrating within the ocean typically between spawning and feeding areas, with migrations that are cyclical and predictable covering more than 100 km. The length at 50% maturity is approximately 16.35 cm (combined sexes). Spawning periods vary and are linked to monsoon seasonality. In Omani waters, peak spawning is observed in October and June–July, and in Indian waters it is primarily post-monsoon (October–January).

Indian oil sardine short morphological description includes dorsal spines 0 and dorsal soft rays 13–21, anal spines 0 and anal soft rays 12–23, and vertebrae 45–49. The body is sub-cylindrical. There is a faint golden spot behind the gill opening, followed by a faint golden mid-lateral line, and a distinct black spot at the hind border of the gill cover (absence of pigment). It is distinguished from all clupeids in the northern Indian Ocean by a pelvic fin ray count of i+8, and distinguished from S. neglecta and S. lemuru by a longer head and more lower gill rakers.

Indian oil sardine human uses include being one of the most economically important fish of India and contributing approximately 10% of total marine fisheries catch of India. It is used fresh, salted, dried, and as fish meal for aquaculture. It is rich in omega-3 PUFAs (EPA and DHA), protein, and vitamins. The lipid content is greater than 8% and it is classified as a high-fat fish. It contains genes for PUFA biosynthesis (Elovl, Fads2 genes). The price category is medium.

Indian oil sardine climate sensitivity note states that it is a model organism for studying climate and fishing impacts on Indian Ocean resources and responds to alterations in oceanographic parameters with localised extinction and recolonisation. Its distribution has been expanding northward since the 1980s in response to rising sea surface temperatures and is strongly linked to SW monsoon upwelling events.
Scomberomorus commerson (narrow-barred Spanish mackerel) belongs to Class Teleostei, Order Scombriformes, Family Scombridae, and Subfamily Scombrinae. Its IUCN status is Near Threatened.

Scomberomorus commerson inhabits marine pelagic-neritic environments in tropical waters, typically at depths of 10–70 m and occasionally up to 200 m. It is an inshore pelagic predator associated with continental shelf edges, drop-offs, sloping reefs, coastal waters, and lagoons, and tolerates turbidity and low salinity. The species may undertake coastal migrations or remain resident, and typically occurs solitarily or in small groups.

Seer fish (Narrow-barred Spanish mackerel) biology includes a maximum length of 240 cm FL (male/unsexed) and a maximum weight of approximately 44.91 kg. The maturity (Lm) is 75.2 cm (range 55–82 cm) with age at first maturity approximately 2 years. The trophic level is an apex predator (estimated approximately 4.5). Resilience is medium, fishing vulnerability is high, and climate vulnerability is moderate. Size notes indicate a common commercial size of 60–100 cm FL.

Seer fish (Narrow-barred Spanish mackerel) distribution includes the Southeast Atlantic at St. Helena and the Indo-West Pacific from the Red Sea and South Africa to Southeast Asia, north to China and Japan, south to southeast Australia, and to Fiji, and is a Lessepsian migrant to the Mediterranean Sea. Key regions for the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal include the entire Indian coastline (both coasts), Arabian Sea, Gulf of Oman, Persian Gulf, Bay of Bengal, and Sri Lanka. It is one of the premier commercial and recreational fish of the Indian subcontinent and is known as Surmai in India.

Scomberomorus commerson is an active visual predator that feeds primarily on smaller schooling fishes such as anchovies, sardines, and Indian mackerel, along with penaeid shrimps and squid. Diet composition varies based on prey availability.

Scomberomorus commerson is a batch spawner that forms spawning aggregations near reef slopes and edges. Spawning season varies with temperature, and females spawn multiple times during a season at intervals of a few days.

Scomberomorus commerson has 15–18 dorsal spines and 15–20 dorsal soft rays, with 16–21 anal soft rays and 42–46 vertebrae. The body is elongate and moderately compressed with a large mouth and sharp compressed teeth. The lateral line bends downward below the second dorsal fin and the swim bladder is absent. Colour is bluish grey above and silvery below with narrow dark vertical bars, and juveniles have oval dark spots.

Scomberomorus commerson is a highly commercial and prized food fish in the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal and is also valued as a gamefish. It has very high market value, though consumption may be associated with ciguatera poisoning in some cases.
Katsuwonus pelamis (skipjack tuna) belongs to Class Teleostei, Order Scombriformes, Family Scombridae, and Subfamily Scombrinae. Its IUCN status is Least Concern.

Katsuwonus pelamis is a marine pelagic-oceanic species found in tropical and warm-temperate waters worldwide, typically from the surface to 260 m depth. It prefers temperatures around 27°C and is commonly found in offshore waters. Larvae occur in surface waters of 15–30°C. The species forms large surface schools associated with birds, drifting objects, sharks, and whales, and exhibits behaviors such as jumping, feeding frenzies, and surface foaming. It is highly migratory and absent from the Black Sea.

Skipjack tuna biology includes a maximum length of 110 cm FL (male/unsexed), a common length of 80.0 cm FL, a maximum weight of 34.5 kg, and a maximum age of 12 years. The maturity (Lm) is 41.3 cm (range 40–45 cm). The trophic level is 4.4 ± 0.5 (based on diet studies). Resilience is medium (population doubling time 1.4–4.4 years), fishing vulnerability is moderate (38 of 100), and climate vulnerability is moderate (41 of 100). Generation time is approximately 2.1 years (1.8–2.4). Preferred temperature is 13.3–29.0°C with mean 26.2°C. Bayesian length-weight parameters are a=0.01072 and b=3.10 (cm total length).

Katsuwonus pelamis is a cosmopolitan species distributed across tropical and warm-temperate waters worldwide, except the Black Sea. It is highly migratory and occurs throughout the year in the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal, where it is one of the most abundant tuna species and a dominant component of Indian Ocean fisheries.

Katsuwonus pelamis is an opportunistic predator that feeds on fishes, crustaceans, cephalopods, and mollusks in the epipelagic zone. Its diet includes small pelagic fishes such as sardines and anchovies, as well as squid and crustaceans, with cannibalism commonly observed.

Katsuwonus pelamis spawns throughout the year in tropical waters as a batch spawner, with reproductively active females spawning frequently and releasing eggs in multiple portions. Eggs and larvae are pelagic.

Katsuwonus pelamis has 14–16 dorsal spines and 14–15 dorsal soft rays, with 14–15 anal soft rays and 41 vertebrae. The body is fusiform and streamlined with small conical teeth and numerous gill rakers. The dorsal and anal fins are followed by finlets, and the body is mostly scaleless except for the corselet. Colour is dark blue above and silvery below with distinct longitudinal dark bands.

Skipjack tuna human uses include fisheries as highly commercial and as a gamefish, and is marketed fresh, frozen, or canned, and also dried-salted and smoked, and is the world's most commercially important tuna species. The price category is high. Nutrients per 100 g wet weight include calcium 155 mg [39–307], iron 2.27 mg [0.81–7.03], protein 22.3% [20.8–23.6], Omega-3 0.45 g [0.22–1.05], selenium 87.4 µg [31.2–237.6], and zinc 1.54 mg [0.37–3.23].
Uroteuthis duvaucelii (Indian squid) belongs to Kingdom Animalia, Phylum Mollusca, Class Cephalopoda, Order Teuthida, and Family Loliginidae.

Uroteuthis duvaucelii is a marine pelagic-neritic and benthopelagic species found on continental shelves at depths of 3–170 m, with highest abundance between 30–120 m. It performs diel vertical migration, staying near the seafloor during the day and ascending to surface waters at night, forming schools that can be targeted by both bottom trawling and light-luring methods. Juveniles occur in shallow nearshore waters, while adults inhabit deeper shelf areas. Continuous presence of small individuals indicates year-round spawning, and monsoon conditions influence growth and seasonal abundance.

Indian squid biology includes a maximum mantle length of 40.0 cm ML (male/unsexed) and a common ML up to 15–16 cm in Indian fisheries with a documented range of 20–309 mm from India. The life span is very short with a maximum of approximately 6 months typical for tropical loliginid squids, and age range in fishery catches is 73–154 days old with sizes 3.1–28.1 cm ML. Age at first maturity is approximately 98 days for males and approximately 106 days for females. Average daily growth is 1.22 mm ML/day for females and 1.48 mm ML/day for males. Maturity (Lm) is 10.0 cm ML (range 9–12.9 cm). Hatch dates are distributed throughout the year with two peaks indicating two cohorts in July and October. Recruitment to trawl fishery occurs at approximately 2 months of age. The sex ratio shows males slightly larger than females. Growth plasticity is very high, and monsoon conditions significantly affect growth trajectories, especially in the first 40 days post-hatching.

Uroteuthis duvaucelii is distributed across the Indo-West Pacific, including the Indian Ocean, Red Sea, Arabian Sea, and Bay of Bengal, extending from Mozambique to the South China Sea and north to Taiwan. It is widely present along the entire Indian coastline.

Uroteuthis duvaucelii is an active carnivorous predator feeding mainly on crustaceans and small fishes, with cannibalism commonly observed. Its diet includes mysids, euphausiids, ostracods, Acetes species, small fishes, and other squid. It captures prey using tentacles and kills with a beak, and is a visual hunter adapted to low-light conditions during diel vertical migration.

Uroteuthis duvaucelii exhibits continuous spawning throughout the year with seasonal peaks in July and October in the Arabian Sea. Eggs are laid as fertilised embryos in benthic egg masses or pelagic clusters and hatch into planktonic paralarvae that migrate to the upper water column. Recruitment to fisheries is rapid, occurring within about 2 months from hatching. The species has a short lifespan of up to 6 months. Management recommendations include closed fishing seasons during peak spawning or hatching periods in October–November or during subsequent recruitment phases to support population sustainability.

Uroteuthis duvaucelii has a slender mantle commonly up to about 150 mm with broad rhomboidal fins extending along roughly half of the mantle length. It possesses four pairs of arms and two tentacles, and its skin contains chromatophores that enable rapid colour change.

Uroteuthis duvaucelii is one of the most commercially important cephalopods in the Indian Ocean, making up about 97% of India's squid catch. It is harvested using bottom trawls, midwater trawls, and squid jigging. Most of the catch is processed into a wide range of products and exported, with major markets including Europe, Japan, and China, while also supporting domestic consumption and contributing significantly to foreign exchange earnings.
Thunnus albacares (yellowfin tuna) belongs to Class Teleostei, Order Scombriformes, Family Scombridae, and Subfamily Scombrinae. Its IUCN status is Least Concern (LC), assessed January 2021.

Thunnus albacares is a marine and occasionally brackish pelagic-oceanic species with an oceanodromous life strategy. It occurs globally between 50°N and 50°S and occupies depths from 1 to 1,602 m, though it is usually found between 1 and 250 m. It inhabits tropical waters with temperatures ranging from 15–31°C and a preferred temperature of 28°C. The species is oceanic and occurs in open water above and below the thermocline, rarely associating with reefs. It forms schools primarily by size in both monospecific and multi-species groups, with larger individuals often associating with porpoises and floating debris. It is sensitive to low oxygen concentrations and is typically not found below 250 m in tropical regions due to the Oxygen Minimum Zone.

Thunnus albacares reaches a maximum length of 239 cm fork length and a common length of 150 cm, with a maximum weight of 200.0 kg and a lifespan of up to 18 years. It matures at about 103.3 cm (range 78–158 cm). The trophic level is 4.4 ± 0.4. It shows medium resilience with a population doubling time of 1.4–4.4 years, moderate to high fishing vulnerability (46 of 100), and moderate climate vulnerability (42 of 100). Generation time is about 2.8 years (range 2.3–3.5). Preferred temperature ranges from 16.5–28.9°C with a mean of 26.7°C. Bayesian length-weight parameters are a=0.01445 and b=3.03 (cm total length).

Thunnus albacares is distributed worldwide in tropical and subtropical seas and is absent from the Mediterranean Sea. It is a highly migratory species. In the Indian Ocean, both the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal serve as major fishing grounds, with particularly large populations occurring in the Arabian Sea, especially off the Indian and Omani coasts.

Thunnus albacares is an opportunistic apex predator feeding on fishes, crustaceans, and squids. Its diet includes small pelagic fishes, cephalopods such as squid, and crustaceans including shrimps and crabs. It feeds on schooling prey in the epipelagic zone, with cannibalism occasionally observed in large aggregations.

Thunnus albacares spawns in tropical and equatorial waters across major oceans. In tropical regions spawning can occur year-round, while at higher latitudes it is seasonal with peaks in summer. It is a batch spawner, releasing eggs every few days during the spawning period. Fertilisation is external, with eggs and sperm released into the water column, and both eggs and larvae are pelagic.

Thunnus albacares has 11–14 dorsal spines and 12–16 dorsal soft rays, with 11–16 anal soft rays and 39 vertebrae. The body is elongate, fusiform, and slightly compressed. The second dorsal and anal fins are very long in large specimens, exceeding 20% of fork length. Pectoral fins are moderately long, measuring 22–31% of fork length. The body has very small scales with a corselet of larger scales. A strong lateral keel is present on the caudal peduncle and a swimbladder is present. Colour is metallic dark blue dorsally, transitioning through yellow to silver ventrally, with dorsal and anal fins and finlets bright yellow with a narrow black border.

Thunnus albacares is a highly commercial species and is also explored in aquaculture. It is primarily caught using longlines and purse seines and is marketed fresh, frozen, canned, and smoked. It is highly valued for sashimi and has a high market price. Nutritional composition per 100 g includes calcium 41 mg, iron 2.84 mg, protein 23.7%, omega-3 fatty acids 0.274 g, selenium 82.4 µg, and zinc 0.524 mg.

Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) advisory background and purpose states that about 7 million people living along the Indian coastline spanning over 8,100 km depend on fishing for their livelihood, and locating and catching fish is always a challenging task where the search often leads to considerable time and resource expenditure, increasing the cost of fishing and leading to low profitability. A reliable and timely advisory on potential zones of fish aggregation benefits the fishing community by reducing time and effort spent searching for fish shoals and improving profitability and socio-economic status of fishers. The Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES), earlier known as the Department of Ocean Development (DOD), initiated the Marine Satellite Information Services (MARSIS) programme in June 1990 to use oceanographic and satellite data to address ocean resource usage. One objective of MARSIS was using remote sensing data for coastal ocean management and harvesting food from the sea, where scientists developed techniques using sea surface temperature (SST) to identify fish aggregation locations. Collaborative efforts resulted in successful satellite-derived demarcation of Potential Fishing Zones (PFZ) as a proxy for fish shoals in Indian waters.

Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) advisory scientific method includes the use of two primary satellite-derived parameters by INCOIS to identify PFZs. Sea surface temperature (SST) is derived from NOAA-AVHRR (USA) and Eumetsat or ESA Met-Op series satellites using thermal-infrared channels, and is used to identify oceanic fronts, meandering patterns, eddies, rings, and upwelling areas as prospective sites for fish accumulation. Thermal fronts concentrate fish because cold upwelled water brings nutrients to the surface, plankton blooms at front boundaries, and fish aggregate at these boundaries to feed. Chlorophyll-a concentration is derived from Oceansat-II (India) and MODIS Aqua (USA) using optical bands and represents phytoplankton biomass where high chlorophyll indicates high primary productivity supporting fish populations. The combined approach uses SST gradients and high chlorophyll zones as strongest indicators of fish aggregation, with wind speed and direction incorporated to account for dynamic shifts in PFZ locations. Upwelling signals include SST drop, nutrient enrichment, phytoplankton bloom, high chlorophyll, and sharp thermal gradients, all processed to locate PFZs.

Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) advisory coverage includes 14 coastal sectors comprising Gujarat, Maharashtra, Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, South Tamil Nadu, North Tamil Nadu, South Andhra Pradesh, North Andhra Pradesh, Odisha (Orissa), West Bengal, Lakshadweep Islands, Andaman Islands, and Nicobar Islands. For each sector, advisories are generated as PFZ maps that include bathymetry, landing centres, and lat/lon, and PFZ text that provides location, depth, distance, and direction from fishing landing centres and lighthouses. All advisories are produced in local or regional languages of each coastal state.

Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) advisory products include PFZ maps containing the location of PFZ with latitude and longitude, major fish landing centres, bathymetry showing water depth contours, and wind speed and direction for drift estimation. PFZ text advisory contains exact coordinates including latitude and longitude, depth at PFZ location, and distance and compass direction from the nearest fish landing centre or lighthouse. SAGAR VANI integrated information dissemination system includes advisories disseminated via multi-lingual SMS messages, voice call or audio advisory, mobile apps with user and admin modules, social media platforms such as Facebook and Twitter or X, email, GTS, fax, electronic display boards at major fishing harbours including 1st Gen LED text, 2nd Gen LCD colour maps, and 3rd Gen advanced utilities under development, as well as radio or television broadcast, IVRS interactive voice response system, and Doordarshan national TV.

Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) advisory coverage and operational details include advisories generated daily except during fishing ban seasons and adverse sea states such as cyclones, high waves, and tsunamis. A total of 586 fish landing centres along the Indian coast receive specific reference advisories, with 3,288 marine fishing villages and 1,511 marine fish landing centres identified in the system. The marine fisher folk population served is 3,999,214, with active fishermen directly targeted at approximately 9,27,120 and direct users currently reached at approximately 3.17 lakh (317,000). Validated impact on catch shows that catch in PFZ areas is substantially higher compared to non-PFZ areas, and advisories are most beneficial to artisanal, motorised, and small mechanised sector fishermen engaged in pelagic fishing such as ring seining and gill netting. There is a reduction in searching time leading to significant fuel savings, reduced human effort, and improved safety. A recent study in 2022–23 along the Kerala coast validated daily PFZ advisories against fish landing records from 117 villages, observing 812 PFZ events during the period, with PFZ events associated with higher catches than non-PFZ events and broader impact over larger spatial extents, confirming that PFZ advisories support sustainable utilisation of localised fish abundance.

Potential Fishing Zones (PFZ) are associated with key oceanographic features including oceanic fronts, upwelling areas, eddies, rings, meandering currents, and chlorophyll bloom zones. Oceanic fronts are boundaries between warm and cold water masses where fish aggregate to feed. Upwelling zones bring cold, nutrient-rich water to the surface, creating high biological productivity. Eddies are rotating current systems where cyclonic eddies enhance upwelling and anti-cyclonic eddies create convergence zones. Rings are detached eddies that retain productive water as they drift. Meandering currents form productive pockets along current boundaries. Chlorophyll bloom zones indicate high phytoplankton biomass and strong food availability for fish.

Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) advisory additional INCOIS ecosystem advisory services include tuna fishing advisory for offshore tuna fishing zones such as Yellowfin and Skipjack using SST, depth of 20°C isotherm, and currents, Hilsa fishery forecast advisory for prediction of Hilsa migration routes and spawning aggregation zones in rivers and the coastal Bay of Bengal, CBAS (Coral Bleaching Alert System) providing SST-based alerts for coral reef thermal stress, ABIS (Algal Bloom Information System) for detection and forecast of harmful algal blooms using chlorophyll-a data, and jellyfish advisory for early warning of jellyfish swarm events affecting fishing gear. Technology and data infrastructure include INCOIS HPC systems processing satellite data in near-real time, Live Access Server (LAS) at https://las.incois.gov.in, ERDDAP at https://erddap.incois.gov.in, Earth System Science Data Portal (ESSDP), and INCOIS serving as the Indian node for IOGOOS (Indian Ocean Global Ocean Observing System) and IIOE-2 (2nd International Indian Ocean Expedition).

Upwelling is the upward movement of cold, nutrient-rich water from deeper layers into the surface euphotic zone and is a key process controlling marine productivity and fisheries yields. It is driven by winds blowing parallel to the coastline, where Ekman transport moves surface water offshore, allowing deeper water from below the thermocline to rise. This results in a decrease in sea surface temperature, increased nutrient concentrations such as nitrates, phosphates, and silicates, and rapid phytoplankton blooms in the euphotic layer. These blooms increase chlorophyll-a concentration, leading to higher zooplankton abundance and aggregation of small pelagic fish such as sardines, anchovies, and mackerel. Predator species such as tuna and seer fish are subsequently attracted, resulting in the formation of Potential Fishing Zones. Upwelling can be detected through low sea surface temperature anomalies, high chlorophyll-a concentration, strong SST gradients, negative sea level anomalies, and positive Ekman pumping velocity indicating upward water flux.

The Arabian Sea exhibits strong seasonal upwelling due to its land-locked northern basin and monsoon-driven wind system, where temperature controls density structure and cold upwelled water creates strong sea surface temperature anomalies. During the southwest monsoon (June–September), peak upwelling occurs driven by the Findlater Jet with wind speeds of 10–15 m/s. Major upwelling zones include the Somali coast where SST drops from approximately 28°C to approximately 14°C forming the Great Whirl with high chlorophyll extending offshore, the Oman coast where SST drops by 5–10°C due to Ekman processes and mixed layer variability, and the southeastern Arabian Sea (Malabar coast, 8°N–14°N) where SST drops by 2–5°C supporting diatom blooms and planktivore fisheries. Peak chlorophyll occurs during August–October. Long-term trends show SST warming of approximately 0.6°C (1971–2020) and chlorophyll decline of –0.32 mg/m³ (1998–2020). During the northeast monsoon (November–February), wind reverses, Somali upwelling weakens, and winter upwelling along the west coast of India occurs with convective mixing enhancing productivity, resulting in a second bloom supporting carnivore fisheries. Inter-monsoon periods include October–November transition and March–May pre-monsoon, with high SST (greater than 30°C) and onset of upwelling along the east coast. The Arabian Sea Oxygen Minimum Zone (150–1,000 m, O2 less than 0.5 ml/l) compresses fish into the upper approximately 250 m, enhancing fish aggregation and PFZ formation.

The Bay of Bengal differs from the Arabian Sea due to strong salinity-controlled stratification, weak sea surface temperature response to upwelling (less than 2°C), lower biological productivity, and absence of a permanent Oxygen Minimum Zone. Surface salinity is reduced (approximately 33 ppt or lower) due to large freshwater input (approximately 1.5 × 10^12 m³ annually) from major rivers including the Ganges, Brahmaputra, Irrawaddy, Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, and Kaveri. This creates low-salinity surface layers (less than 32 ppt), strong stratification, shallow mixed layers, suppressed Ekman pumping, and weak SST signals. Seasonal patterns include pre-monsoon upwelling onset (March–May), weak intermittent upwelling during southwest monsoon (June–July), and northeast monsoon (November–February) open-ocean upwelling with the Sri Lanka Dome producing chlorophyll peaks in February. Freshwater plumes generate high chlorophyll concentrations (approximately 6 mg/m³ near Irrawaddy and approximately 4 mg/m³ near Ganges–Brahmaputra). Fall inter-monsoon (October–November) also shows increased chlorophyll. Fisheries are dominated by planktivores such as hilsa, anchovies, and Indian mackerel, with relatively weaker tuna presence. PFZ detection relies more on modelled SST and chlorophyll due to frequent cloud cover limiting satellite observations.

Seasonal oceanographic patterns differ significantly between the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal. During January–February (winter), the Arabian Sea experiences northeast monsoon conditions with convective mixing, surface cooling, and phytoplankton bloom in the northern Arabian Sea, while the Bay of Bengal shows cyclonic wind curl causing southwest Bay of Bengal upwelling, chlorophyll maximum in February, and strong freshwater influence from river plumes. During March–May (pre-monsoon), the Arabian Sea undergoes strong warming with Arabian Sea Mini Warm Pool formation (SST greater than 30°C), weakening upwelling, and shifting winds, while the Bay of Bengal shows onset of coastal upwelling along the east coast with rising SST and strengthening southwest winds. During June–September (southwest monsoon), the Arabian Sea exhibits peak upwelling across Somali, Oman, and Kerala coasts with SST drops of 5–14°C, strong phytoplankton blooms, peak sardine and anchovy fisheries, tuna aggregation, Oxygen Minimum Zone compression, and maximum PFZ activity, while the Bay of Bengal shows weak and intermittent upwelling masked by salinity stratification, low SST signal, Hilsa spawning migration, and productivity driven mainly by river plumes. During October–November (post-monsoon), the Arabian Sea experiences weakening winds, re-stratification, northeast monsoon onset, fish dispersal, and SST recovery, while the Bay of Bengal shows transition conditions with northeast monsoon onset, divergence-driven blooms in the southwest Bay of Bengal, and Sri Lanka Dome activity.

INCOIS serves as the Indian institutional node for international upwelling and ocean research programmes, including IIOE-2 (Second International Indian Ocean Expedition), hosted at https://iioe-2.incois.gov.in. Under IIOE-2, major upwelling research themes include WIOURI (Western Indian Ocean Upwelling Research Initiative) and EIOURI, covering nine key upwelling systems: Agulhas Current-driven upwelling, Mozambique Channel upwelling, Madagascar Ridge and seamount upwelling, southeast Madagascar shelf (SICC chlorophyll bloom), East African Coastal Current upwelling, Somali Current system upwelling, Oman/Arabian Sea upwelling system, Chagos-Seychelles upwelling dome, and Mascarene Plateau-induced upwelling. The Indian Ocean Global Ocean Observing System (IOGOOS) provides real-time ocean observations including temperature, salinity, and current data, supporting upwelling monitoring and improving PFZ forecasts. The Ocean State Forecast (OSF) system provides 5-day forecasts of sea surface temperature, significant wave height, ocean currents, and wind fields, enabling advance planning for fishing operations and PFZ advisory usage.

Upwelling and seasonal oceanography key parameters used in PFZ and upwelling monitoring include SST measured from NOAA-AVHRR, MetOp or AVHRR, and MODIS where cold anomaly indicates upwelling signal, chlorophyll-a from Oceansat-II and MODIS-Aqua representing phytoplankton biomass as the base of the food chain, sea level anomaly from altimetry such as the Jason series where negative SLA indicates upwelling and positive indicates downwelling, sea surface salinity from Argo floats and SMOS satellite used for monitoring river freshwater influx in the Bay of Bengal, Ekman pumping velocity from reanalysis winds such as ERA5 and NCEP indicating upwelling driver strength, mixed layer depth from Argo floats representing thermocline depth and nutrient access, and dissolved oxygen from INCOIS cruises using CTD or Rosette indicating OMZ boundaries in the Arabian Sea.

Dissolved oxygen in the ocean headline definition states that about half of Earth's oxygen comes from the ocean and about the same amount is consumed by marine life, and core explanation describes that roughly half of oxygen production on Earth comes from the ocean, mainly from oceanic plankton including drifting plants, algae, and some bacteria that photosynthesize. One species, Prochlorococcus, is the smallest photosynthetic organism on Earth and produces up to 20% of the oxygen in the entire biosphere, exceeding all tropical rainforests combined. Calculating exact oxygen production is difficult as it changes constantly, and scientists use satellite imagery to track photosynthesizing plankton and estimate ocean photosynthesis, though satellites cannot capture the full picture. Plankton levels vary seasonally and with nutrient load, temperature, and other factors, and oxygen levels vary with time of day and tides.

The ocean maintains an oxygen balance where it produces at least 50% of Earth's oxygen while roughly the same amount is consumed by marine life. Oxygen production occurs primarily through photosynthesis by phytoplankton in the sunlit euphotic zone, along with contributions from seagrasses, kelp, and algae in coastal and benthic environments, and through gas exchange at the ocean surface where oxygen from the atmosphere dissolves into seawater. Oxygen consumption occurs through respiration by marine animals, cellular respiration by both plants and animals, and microbial decomposition by bacteria that consume oxygen while breaking down dead organic matter on the ocean floor. Most of the oxygen in the Earth's atmosphere has accumulated over hundreds of millions of years, and this accumulated oxygen forms the majority of the oxygen currently available for human respiration.

Dissolved oxygen in the ocean hypoxia describes that when algal blooms die and decomposition uses oxygen faster than it can be replenished, areas of extremely low oxygen concentrations known as hypoxia form, often called dead zones because oxygen levels are too low to support most marine life. The formation process includes excessive nutrients entering the water from agriculture runoff, sewage, and river input, leading to algal blooms or eutrophication, followed by algae dying and sinking to the bottom, where bacteria decompose the algae and consume oxygen, causing oxygen depletion faster than replenishment from the surface, resulting in hypoxic or anoxic conditions where fish, crabs, oysters, and other marine animals die or flee the area. NOAA's National Centers for Coastal Ocean Science (NCCOS) conducts research and forecasting on algal blooms and hypoxia to reduce harm to ocean ecosystems and human environments.

Dissolved oxygen in the ocean nutrient pollution and oxygen depletion describes that excessive amounts of nutrients can lead to serious problems such as low levels of dissolved oxygen in the water, where severe algal growth blocks light needed for plants such as seagrasses to grow, and when algae and seagrass die they decay, using up oxygen in the water and leading to low dissolved oxygen levels that can kill fish, crabs, oysters, and other aquatic animals. Dissolved oxygen and pelagic fish distributions describes that numbers and distributions of pelagic fish species vary regionally and vertically depending on availability of light, nutrients, dissolved oxygen, temperature, salinity, and pressure, and dissolved oxygen is a first-order determinant of where fish can and cannot live in the water column.

document: An Oxygen Minimum Zone (OMZ) is a persistent layer in the ocean water column where dissolved oxygen concentrations are extremely low, typically less than 0.5 ml/L or less than 22 µmol/kg. OMZs form when high biological productivity at the surface produces sinking organic matter that is decomposed by bacteria at depths of 150–1,000 m, consuming oxygen with limited replenishment from surface gas exchange or deep-water ventilation, resulting in persistent oxygen depletion. The Arabian Sea OMZ is among the most intense globally, occurring approximately between 150–1,000 m with oxygen levels often less than 0.1 ml/L, driven by monsoon upwelling and high productivity, and is both permanent and expanding. This OMZ compresses Yellowfin tuna into the upper 0–250 m due to their sensitivity below approximately 0.5 ml/L, increasing surface accessibility, and restricts skipjack tuna larvae to surface waters. Denitrification occurs within OMZ regions, influencing nitrogen and carbon cycles. In contrast, the Bay of Bengal does not have a permanent OMZ but may experience seasonal hypoxia near river mouths. INCOIS monitors dissolved oxygen using CTD or Rosette systems and tracks OMZ boundaries for applications such as tuna habitat modelling.

document: Dissolved oxygen levels determine marine habitat suitability based on defined thresholds. Well-oxygenated conditions occur at greater than 6.0 ml/L, supporting healthy conditions for all marine life. Slightly reduced oxygen levels between 4.0–6.0 ml/L are tolerated by most species. Hypoxic conditions occur at less than 2.0 ml/L, where fish begin to leave the area or experience mortality. Severely hypoxic conditions at less than 0.5 ml/L support very few organisms, with most fish dying and only anaerobic microbes surviving. OMZ or anoxic conditions occur at less than 0.1 ml/L, where virtually no macrofauna can exist. NOAA uses both mg/L and ml/L units, with a conversion of approximately 1 ml/L equal to approximately 1.43 mg/L.

document: Ocean salinity headline definition describes that ocean salt primarily comes from rocks on land and openings in the seafloor, and salinity is the concentration of salt in seawater with an average of about 35 parts per thousand or 3.5% by weight. Core explanation describes that ocean salinity originates from runoff from land where slightly acidic rainwater erodes rocks releasing ions that are transported by rivers to the ocean, and from hydrothermal vents where seawater interacts with magma-heated rocks, losing oxygen, magnesium, and sulfates while gaining metals such as iron, zinc, and copper before being released back into the ocean. Additional sources include underwater volcanic eruptions releasing minerals and salt domes formed over geological timescales found under land and sea.

The chemical composition of ocean salinity is dominated by dissolved ions, with chloride (Cl⁻) and sodium (Na⁺) together accounting for about 85% of all dissolved ions, forming sodium chloride. Magnesium and sulfate contribute about 10%, while all other ions are present in very small concentrations. Salinity variation is controlled by temperature, evaporation, and precipitation. Higher temperatures increase evaporation, which removes water and concentrates salt, raising salinity, while precipitation adds freshwater and dilutes salinity. Global salinity patterns show low salinity at the equator due to high rainfall and river runoff, low salinity at the poles due to ice melt and low evaporation, and high salinity at mid-latitudes around 30°N and 30°S due to high evaporation and low rainfall. The average ocean salinity is about 35 ppt, equivalent to 35 g of salt per 1 kg of seawater or 3.5% by weight.

Ocean salinity is influenced by freshwater input from rivers, where rain replenishes freshwater in rivers and streams, keeping them non-saline, while oceans accumulate salts and minerals from all rivers flowing into them. Rivers from the United States alone discharge about 225 million tons of dissolved solids and 513 million tons of suspended sediment annually into the ocean, and globally rivers transport approximately 4 billion tons of dissolved salts each year. This input is balanced by the deposition of similar amounts of salt as sediment on the ocean floor, maintaining relatively stable ocean salinity over geological time. Salinity is measured using CTD rosette instruments that record conductivity, temperature, and depth to derive salinity, and through satellite-based observations using NASA SMOS and Aquarius or SAC-D missions to measure surface salinity from space.

document: Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal salinity application notes describe that the Arabian Sea is a high salinity basin with approximately 35.5–36.5 ppt in the open ocean due to high evaporation and limited freshwater input from surrounding arid regions, with extreme salinity in the Persian Gulf exceeding 40 ppt. High salinity means density is primarily temperature-controlled, enabling strong SST signals during upwelling and contributing to thermohaline circulation with formation of intermediate water masses such as Persian Gulf Water and Red Sea Water. In contrast, the Bay of Bengal is a low salinity basin with approximately 32–34 ppt in the north and sometimes less than 30 ppt near river mouths due to very high freshwater input from rivers including Ganges, Brahmaputra, Irrawaddy, Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, and Kaveri totaling approximately 1.5 × 10¹² m³ annually, combined with lower evaporation. This creates a halocline that forms a barrier layer, suppresses vertical mixing, reduces SST upwelling signals, and dominates density stratification, with lowest salinity during September–October and recovery during February–April. Salinity influences fish such as Hilsa which are euryhaline and use salinity gradients for spawning migration, and prawns such as Penaeus monodon which prefer 15–25 ppt but tolerate 2–34 ppt with juveniles in estuaries and adults offshore. The low-salinity surface lens in the Bay of Bengal separates coastal and offshore ecosystems, and CTD salinity measurements are used by INCOIS to monitor halocline structure and mixed layer depth for PFZ advisories.

document: A thermocline is the transition layer in the ocean between warmer mixed surface water and colder deep water below. Ocean water is vertically structured based on temperature, with the upper epipelagic or sunlight zone interacting with wind and waves that mix and distribute heat. The thermocline forms at the base of this mixed layer, where temperature decreases rapidly with depth, creating a sharp boundary between warm surface water and much colder deep water. This makes the thermocline a distinct and important layer in the ocean water column.

Thermocline temperature profile with depth describes ocean layering where the epipelagic zone extends from the surface to approximately 200 m with warm, wind-mixed, and relatively uniform temperature interacting with the atmosphere, followed by the thermocline or mesopelagic zone from approximately 200 m to approximately 1,000 m where temperature decreases rapidly with depth forming a transition layer, then deep water from approximately 1,000 m to approximately 4,000 m where temperature remains relatively constant, and the abyssal zone below approximately 4,000 m where temperatures are near freezing to just above freezing. Conversions include 3,300 feet approximately equal to 1,005 m and 13,100 feet approximately equal to 3,993 m.

Thermocline seasonal and geographic variation describes that in tropical regions such as the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal, a semi-permanent thermocline exists year-round at approximately 50–150 m depth due to consistently warm surface waters, and becomes stronger and shallower during upwelling events as the thermocline shoals toward the surface. In temperate regions, the thermocline is variable, typically deepest during summer due to surface heating and weakening or disappearing in winter due to mixing by cooling and storms. In polar regions, the thermocline is shallow or nonexistent because the water column is uniformly cold with little temperature contrast. Thermocline and hurricane forecasting describes that the depth of warm water above the thermocline determines the available energy or fuel tank for hurricanes, as water vapor from warm ocean surfaces fuels storms, and deeper warm layers increase storm intensity potential. Similarly, oceanographic applications include the use of thermocline depth or isotherms such as 26°C and 20°C (D20) by INCOIS in tuna fishing advisories, as tuna tend to congregate above the thermocline where prey is concentrated.

Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal thermocline application notes describe that the Arabian Sea has a strong semi-permanent thermocline typical of tropical oceans, which shoals during southwest monsoon upwelling to as shallow as 20–50 m along the Kerala coast, bringing nutrient-rich sub-thermocline water into the euphotic zone and triggering phytoplankton blooms, and deepens again during post-monsoon as surface warming and reduced winds occur, with the 20°C isotherm (D20) used as a proxy for thermocline depth in fisheries forecasting. In the Bay of Bengal, the thermocline is also semi-permanent but overlain by a barrier layer of low-salinity freshwater from rivers such as the Ganges–Brahmaputra and Irrawaddy, which suppresses vertical mixing, prevents thermocline erosion, and weakens upwelling SST signals, with shallow mixed layer depth of approximately 20–30 m compared to approximately 50–70 m in the Arabian Sea. Thermocline and fish behaviour describes that Yellowfin tuna inhabit waters above the thermocline and are constrained by the oxygen minimum zone below, creating a compressed habitat that enhances catchability, while skipjack tuna larvae remain in surface waters of 15–30°C above the thermocline, and sardines, anchovies, and mackerel occupy the mixed layer and track thermocline depth seasonally.

document: Halocline and pycnocline related concept describes that a halocline is the depth at which salinity changes most rapidly in the ocean, acting as the salinity equivalent of the thermocline, and in the Bay of Bengal a strong near-surface halocline is formed due to freshwater river input, reinforcing density stratification and creating a barrier layer above the thermocline. The pycnocline is the depth of maximum density change, which in most oceans coincides with the thermocline because temperature controls density, but in the Bay of Bengal the pycnocline is shallower than the thermocline because salinity rather than temperature dominates surface density structure.

Upwelling headline definition describes upwelling as a process in which deep, cold water rises toward the surface. Core explanation describes that winds blowing across the ocean surface push water away, and deeper water rises to replace it, occurring both in the open ocean and along coastlines, while the reverse process called downwelling occurs when surface water accumulates and sinks. Upwelled water is colder and rich in nutrients, fertilizing surface waters and leading to high biological productivity, which makes upwelling regions some of the most productive fishing grounds.

Upwelling expanded explanation describes that winds blowing across the ocean surface push water away from an area, causing deeper water to rise and replace the displaced surface water, which is the process of upwelling, and conditions are optimal along coasts when winds blow parallel to the shoreline. Upwelling occurs both in the open ocean and along coastlines, while the reverse process, downwelling, occurs when surface water accumulates and sinks. The subsurface water that rises during upwelling is colder, nutrient-rich, and highly biologically productive, making such regions among the most productive fishing grounds, as seen along the west coasts of Africa and South America. Seasonal patterns are also observed, such as along the west coast of the United States where winter winds cause downwelling and summer winds cause offshore transport and coastal upwelling, leading to cold coastal waters and phenomena such as summer fog in regions like San Francisco.

Upwelling related NOAA facts describe that during normal Pacific Ocean conditions, trade winds blow westward along the equator, moving warm water away from South America and allowing cold, nutrient-rich water to rise through upwelling. During El Niño, trade winds weaken, warm water shifts eastward, and upwelling weakens or stops, reducing nutrient supply, phytoplankton, and consequently fish populations. During La Niña, stronger trade winds enhance upwelling, increasing nutrient supply and biological productivity. The ocean gets colder at depth because cold water is denser than warm water, causing cold, salty water to sink below warmer surface water, forming part of the global conveyor belt circulation system. In coastal regions, wind-driven currents can locally drive processes such as coastal upwelling.

Upwelling Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal application notes describe that winds pushing surface water away correspond to the southwest monsoon Findlater Jet in the Arabian Sea, where alongshore winds drive Ekman transport offshore and cause coastal upwelling along the Somali, Omani, and Kerala coasts. The upwelled water originates from below the thermocline at approximately 100–200 m depth, is colder with SST drops of 5–14°C, and is nutrient-rich, leading to phytoplankton blooms and high chlorophyll detectable by satellites. These productive upwelling zones form the scientific basis for INCOIS PFZ advisories, with regions along the southwest Indian coast such as Kerala and Karnataka being among the most productive fishing grounds. Downwelling processes are also important, particularly in the Bay of Bengal during the northeast monsoon when winds push surface water toward the east coast of India, causing downwelling and reduced productivity during winter.
